nativeRange.habitat: Found in blackwater rivers and tributaries where the water is tannin-stained, warm, soft, and acidic. The riverbed is typically covered in leaf litter, and the banks are lined with dense vegetation whose roots and overhanging growth provide shade and shelter.

decorPreferences: dense planting or floating plants for diffused light
driftwood and leaf litter for tannins

diet.notes: Accepts most small prepared foods including nano pellets, micro flakes, and frozen or live foods such as baby brine shrimp, daphnia, and bloodworms. Small mouth requires appropriately sized food particles. Color-enhancing foods can help maintain vivid red coloration.

avoidWith: boisterous or fast-moving fish causing stress
fish with mouths large enough to consume them

imageCandidates.notes: Gavinevans; 2021x1560; group of several H. rhodostomus showing the schooling behavior this species is kept for, with red heads and black-and-white striped caudal fins clearly visible on multiple individuals.
Bernat Arlandis; 3552x2496; tack-sharp lone specimen, vivid red head and crisp black-and-white striped tail against clean bokeh — best single-fish diagnostic shot.
Tomasz Lachowicz; 1366x768; sharp full-body side profile, vibrant red nose and striped caudal fin, different angle in a blackwater-style aquascape.

summary: Iconic schooling tetra with a vivid red head and black-and-white striped tail; tends to school tightly and is often used as a natural water-quality indicator since the red coloration fades noticeably in poor conditions.
careNotes:
The Rummy Nose Tetra is widely regarded as one of the most tightly schooling freshwater fish available in the hobby — groups of 10 or more tend to move in close, synchronized formation, pivoting together as a unit rather than milling loosely. The vivid red head coloration has been observed to fade noticeably in response to elevated nitrates, ammonia, or pH swings, making this species a useful informal water-quality indicator. Regular partial water changes are generally advised to maintain both color and long-term health.

Three species are commonly sold under the name "Rummy Nose Tetra": Hemigrammus rhodostomus (the true rummy nose, most frequently seen in the trade), Hemigrammus bleheri (the firehead tetra, distinguished by red coloring that extends further down the body), and Petitella georgiae (the false rummy nose, distinguishable by differences in the lateral line pattern and caudal fin markings). Husbandry requirements are similar across all three, and H. rhodostomus is the species most commonly encountered at retail.

This species tends to prefer the soft, acidic blackwater conditions of its native lower Amazon and Orinoco basin habitat. While it has been reported to adapt to slightly harder water, keeping pH below 7.0 and hardness low generally yields better coloration and long-term health. Dimmed lighting, a dark substrate, and the addition of driftwood or botanical leaf litter are commonly recommended to replicate natural conditions and reduce stress.

The Rummy Nose Tetra can opportunistically consume very small fry and newly hatched shrimp, but is generally considered safe with adult dwarf shrimp. Freshly hatched or juvenile shrimp may be at risk.
breedingNotes: The Rummy Nose Tetra is generally considered difficult to breed in captivity. It is an egg scatterer that tends to spawn in the evening hours, and a separate breeding tank is commonly recommended. That tank should have very soft, acidic water (pH below 6.5), minimal lighting, a fine mesh bottom or spawning mop to catch and protect eggs from the adults, and a gentle sponge filter. Adults are known to consume their own eggs and should generally be removed from the breeding tank once spawning is complete. Eggs typically hatch within 24 to 36 hours. The fry are very small at first and may require infusoria (microscopic organisms cultured for tiny fish larvae) or commercially available micro fry foods before they are large enough to accept baby brine shrimp. Sexing outside of breeding condition is difficult; females tend to appear slightly rounder in the body when carrying eggs.

additional.name: SeriouslyFish
additional.notes: Primary cross-verification source. Confirmed scientific name, size 5 cm, temperature 24–27°C, pH 5.5–7.0, gH 2–15, tank length 90 cm for 10+ fish, mid-water swim zone, omnivorous diet, peaceful temperament, minimum group 10. Also confirmed three-species trade confusion and tight schooling behavior.

additional.name: Fishkeeping World
additional.notes: Secondary cross-verification. Confirmed omnivore, peaceful, schooling, min group 6 (used 8 as averaged conservative value). Gave temperature 75–84°F (24–29°C) and pH 6.2–7.0; lifespan 6–8 years; kH 2–10 (used 1–5 per blackwater species brief). Minor disagreement on pH minimum (6.2 vs 5.5); retained 5.5 as more consistent with blackwater biotope per SeriouslyFish and species brief.
